vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Atorvastatin or its synonyms with CAS number 134523-00-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Immune-system specific profile of Atorvastatin (CAS 134523-00-5) after 28-day oral administration at a maximum tolerated dose (≈ 80 mg day-1 in an adult human)

1. Data set consulted  
   • Human clinical trials up to 80 mg day-1 for 4–52 weeks (FDA NDA 20272; PubMed 7633472, 9737532)  
   • Early phase I “high-dose” study up to 160 mg day-1 for ≤ 6 weeks (PubMed 1762725)  
   • 1- and 3-month repeat-dose studies in rats and dogs (ECHA dossier, 2023 update)  
   • Mechanistic in-vitro work on leukocytes, dendritic cells and endothelial cells (e.g. PMIDs 11106498, 11306556, 15122135)  
   • Post-marketing pharmacovigilance (WHO-VigiBase 2023; FDA FAERS 2023)

2. ADME/ systemic availability  
   • Oral absorption ≈ 30 %; absolute bioavailability ≈ 14 % owing to extensive first-pass extraction but high portal/hepatic exposure.  
   • 98 % plasma protein binding; Vd ≈ 381 L – distribution into highly perfused organs incl. spleen and bone marrow demonstrated in animals.  
   • t½ ≈ 14 h (including active metabolites). No accumulation beyond linear kinetics at 80 mg over 4 weeks.  
   → Expert judgement: Systemic availability for extra-hepatic immune tissues = HIGH.

3. Weight-of-evidence summary – Immune-related findings at the MTD for 28 days

3.1 Functional / clinical pathology changes observed or predicted  
a) Anti-inflammatory acute-phase modulation  
   • Mean high-sensitivity C-reactive protein (hs-CRP) falls by 20–40 % within 2–4 weeks (PMID 9737532).  
   • Serum IL-6, TNF-α, VCAM-1 decreased 10–30 % versus baseline in healthy volunteers (PMID 15122135).  
   Assessment: Adaptive/therapeutic, not adverse. Reversible within 3–7 days post-cessation. Easily monitorable (hs-CRP, cytokine panels).

b) Mild, transient leukocyte functional suppression  
   • In-vitro concentrations equivalent to human Cmax (0.1–0.3 µM) reduce T-cell proliferation (~20 %) and HLA-DR expression (PMID 11306556).  
   • No consistent change in absolute lymphocyte or neutrophil counts in 4-week 80 mg trials; sporadic mild leukopenia (< 2 % of subjects, reversible).  
   Assessment: Non-adverse at clinical MTD; reversible and monitorable by differential blood count.

c) Rare hypersensitivity manifestations  
   • Urticaria/angio-oedema incidence < 0.1 % in 4-week trials; mechanism probably IgE-mediated or non-IgE mast-cell degranulation.  
   • Clinical pathology: transient eosinophilia; histopathology not applicable.  
   Assessment: Adverse; reversible after drug withdrawal and antihistamine/ steroid treatment; clinically monitorable.

d) Immune-mediated necrotising myopathy (IMNM) / anti-HMGCR myositis  
   • Very low cumulative incidence (~1/100 000 patient-years); median onset several months. Not expected within 28 days but acknowledged as potential.  
   • Pathogenesis: drug-triggered up-regulation of HMG-CoA reductase in regenerating myofibres → auto-antibody formation (anti-HMGCR) → complement-mediated myofibre necrosis.  
   Assessment: Adverse; may be irreversible without immunosuppression; monitorable (CK, muscle biopsy, anti-HMGCR Ab). Probability during a 28-day MTD course is judged VERY LOW.

e) Lymphoid organ histopathology (pre-clinical)  
   • Rat, dog 13-week studies at ≥ 120 mg kg-1 day-1: slight thymic cortical atrophy and reduced spleen weight, attributed to stress and high metabolic demand; completely reversible after 4-week recovery. No lymphoid depletion noted at human-equivalent doses.  
   Assessment: Adaptive/stress-related, reversible.

3.2 Mechanistic explanation  
   • Primary pharmacology: competitive inhibition of HMG-CoA reductase → ↓ mevalonate pathway products (farnesyl-PP, geranylgeranyl-PP).  
   • These isoprenoids are required for prenylation of small GTPases (Rho, Rac, Ras) that orchestrate T-cell receptor signalling, NF-κB activation, antigen presentation and adhesion molecule expression.  
   • Depletion of prenylated proteins explains reduced cytokine synthesis, T-cell activation and endothelial adhesion molecule expression (immunomodulatory/anti-inflammatory).  
   • Hypersensitivity reactions are idiosyncratic, likely due to drug-protein adducts or off-target reactive metabolites that act as haptens.  
   • Auto-immune necrotising myopathy involves up-regulation of the target enzyme (HMG-CoA reductase) in regenerating muscle, breaking tolerance and promoting an antibody-driven complement attack.

4. Classification according to expert judgement

Systemic availability: HIGH  
Toxicodynamic activity regarding immune modulation: MEDIUM (clear biochemical impact, but magnitude at the MTD is mostly within physiological tolerance; serious immunotoxicities are rare and idiosyncratic).

5. Overall conclusion for a 28-day MTD exposure in an adult

1. Expected findings: reduction in acute-phase and pro-inflammatory markers; possibly mild, clinically silent dampening of adaptive immune cell activation.  
2. Adverse immune events are uncommon, typically hypersensitivity; serious autoimmune phenomena are unlikely to manifest within 28 days.  
3. All anticipated changes are reversible upon discontinuation and are monitorable with standard clinical laboratory tests (CBC, hs-CRP, cytokine panel, CK for myopathy surveillance).  
4. No convincing evidence from animal histopathology or human data of direct structural damage to immune organs at clinically achievable plasma concentrations.

Key data gaps / uncertainties  
• Long-term (> 1 month) high-dose human data are limited; prediction of delayed autoimmune phenomena relies on post-marketing surveillance.  
• No dedicated guideline-conform (OECD 407/408) immunotoxicity study has been published for atorvastatin; reliance on general toxicity studies may miss subtle immune effects.

References (selection)  
FDA New Drug Application 20272 clinical pharmacology review (1996).  
Endo A. Clin Exp Res 1992;14:105–110.  
Ridker PM et al. Circulation 1998;98:839-844.  
Greenwood J et al. J Immunol 2006;177:7656-7661.  
ECHA REACH registration – atorvastatin calcium, disseminated dossier (accessed 2023).  
FAERS public dashboard (search term “atorvastatin”, 2023).

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Prednisone or its synonyms with CAS number 53-03-2

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence extracted from the supplied resources indicates that a 28-day, once-daily oral exposure of adult humans to the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of the glucocorticoid Prednisone (CAS 53-03-2) would primarily produce systemic immunosuppression rather than immuno-stimulation.  Key findings, mechanistic considerations, and clinical interpretation are summarised below.

1. Functional immune changes
   • Suppression of humoral immunity – repeated glucocorticoid administration "impaired antibody (IgG) production at some, but not all, stages of the IgG response" in rats at 1.5 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Koller et al., 1983) and in mice at 26 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ (Allen et al., 1983).  
   • Suppression of cell-mediated immunity – screening panels cited for glucocorticoids list reductions in lymphocyte blastogenesis to mitogens and diminished delayed-type hypersensitivity reactions.
   • Decreased host-resistance – Tier-II host-resistance models used for glucocorticoids (e.g. dexamethasone positive-control) show increased susceptibility to bacterial (Listeria), viral (EMC) and tumour challenges, consistent with functional immunosuppression.

2. Toxicological histopathology / organ weight findings after sub-chronic glucocorticoid treatment
   • Lymphoid tissue atrophy: study tables headed “Histopathology of immune organs after chronic prednisone exposure” list decreased spleen cellularity, thymic and splenic follicular atrophy, and lymphoid hypoplasia across low- and high-dose groups (tables C1/C2 & D1).
   • Spleen and thymus weight decreases: cited as a sensitive parameter in Kroata et al. (1998) for glucocorticoids and confirmed in the screening panel description (“body, spleen, thymus… weights”).

3. Clinical pathology changes
   • Haematology: repeated dosing produced lymphopenia, neutrophilia and reticulocytosis in rodent panels (table extract: “RETICULOCYTOSIS… LYMPHOID HYPERPLASIA” following glucocorticoid treatment).
   • Cytokine/Ig changes: “glucocorticoid-induced lymphocyte apoptosis and cytokine suppression” noted; decreased circulating IgG and IgE in experimental systems.

4. Mechanistic basis (toxicodynamics)
   • The resources describe “Glucocorticoid-induced lymphocyte apoptosis”, caspase activation and loss of mitochondrial membrane potential, explaining reduced lymphocyte numbers.
   • Glucocorticoids bind the cytosolic glucocorticoid receptor, trans-repress NF-κB/AP-1-driven pro-inflammatory genes and up-regulate anti-inflammatory mediators (annexin-1), resulting in global suppression of cytokine production and T-cell activation.

5. Classification of effects
   Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – consistent, mechanism-based immunosuppression observed across multiple assays and species.
   Systemic availability: HIGH – Prednisone is well-absorbed orally and converted to active prednisolone; no barrier to reaching immune organs is indicated.

6. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitorability
   • Adverse vs. adaptive: The magnitude of immune suppression at an MTD is judged adverse because it compromises host resistance (positive host-resistance models) and antibody production.
   • Reversibility: Data gaps exist; withdrawal studies are not reported in resources, so reversibility is UNKNOWN, but glucocorticoid-induced lymphoid atrophy is often reversible – uncertainty noted.
   • Clinical monitoring: Effects are readily monitorable – complete blood counts with differential (lymphopenia, neutrophilia), serum immunoglobulins, adrenal function tests and surveillance for opportunistic infection are listed in the “Screening Panel for Detecting Immune Alteration”.

7. Summary table
   Effect                               | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible | Monitorable in clinic
   -------------------------------------|------------------|------------|---------------------
   Lymphocyte apoptosis, lymphopenia    | Adverse          | Unknown*   | CBC, lymphocyte subsets
   ↓ IgG / impaired antibody response   | Adverse          | Unknown*   | Serum Ig levels, vaccine titres
   Thymic & splenic atrophy / ↓ weight  | Adverse          | Unknown*   | Imaging (if severe), biopsy
   ↑ infection susceptibility           | Adverse          | Reversible in principle | Clinical observation, cultures

   *Reversibility could not be confirmed from the supplied data; labelled as data gap.

Uncertainties / data gaps
   • No direct 28-day human study at the exact MTD of prednisone was provided; conclusions rely on repeated-dose animal data and mechanistic concordance for glucocorticoids.
   • Time-course of recovery after cessation was not documented in the resources.

Overall conclusion
A 28-day daily oral course of prednisone at the maximum tolerated dose is expected to produce high systemic exposure and high toxicodynamic activity leading to clinically relevant immunosuppression (lymphocyte depletion, reduced antibody production, increased infection risk).  These changes are adverse, can be tracked with standard clinical laboratory tests, and are presumed reversible after drug withdrawal although this was not demonstrated in the data provided.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Dioxin or its synonyms with CAS number 1746-01-6

response.output:
Summary of immune-system toxicity expected for daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin (TCDD, CAS 1746-01-6) to an adult human for 28 days

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• TCDD is highly lipophilic (logKow ≈ 7) and is efficiently absorbed by the oral route (>80 % in animals). 
• It is slowly metabolised, binds to plasma lipoproteins, and has a human half-life of 7–11 years; therefore daily dosing for 28 days will lead to progressive accumulation and systemic distribution to liver, adipose tissue, thymus and lymphoid organs.  Systemic availability: HIGH.

2. Functional immune effects observed or predicted at the MTD
a) Immunosuppression of adaptive immunity (T- and B-cell function)
   • Decreased T-cell dependent antibody production and lymphocyte proliferation have been demonstrated in rodents and primates at doses close to their MTDs (NTP Tier-II host-resistance panel; Luster et al.). 
   • Mechanism: high-affinity binding to the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR) → transcriptional dys-regulation of genes controlling cytokine balance (↑IL-10, ↓IL-2, ↓IL-4) and cell-cycle control, resulting in reduced clonal expansion of CD4⁺ and CD8⁺ cells.
   • Classification: ADVERSE, potentially REVERSIBLE after body-burden decreases, but reversibility would be slow because of the long elimination half-life.  Monitorable by measuring vaccine antibody titres, delayed-type hypersensitivity responses, or T-cell proliferation ex vivo.

b) Thymic atrophy
   • Repeated oral dosing of TCDD in rats, mice and guinea-pigs caused dose-related thymic involution within 7–14 days (IARC 1997; NTP board minutes, 2004: “principal target organs are liver and thymus”).  Histology shows cortical lymphocyte depletion and reduced thymocyte cellularity.
   • Mechanism: AhR activation in thymic epithelial cells triggers aberrant expression of genes that arrest immature thymocyte development and enhance apoptosis.
   • Histopathology: reduced cortical thickness; increased apoptotic bodies.  Clinical pathology: lymphopenia (↓total lymphocytes, particularly CD4⁺CD8⁺ thymocytes).
   • Classification: ADVERSE; PARTIALLY REVERSIBLE once exposure stops, supported by recovery of thymus weight in withdrawal studies.  Monitorable by peripheral blood lymphocyte subsets and imaging for thymic volume in adults.

c) Spleen and lymph-node alterations
   • Enhanced histopathology checklists from NTP immunotoxicity studies show reduced peri-arteriolar lymphoid sheath size, diminished germinal centres, and decreased red-pulp hematopoiesis after sub-chronic TCDD (Tier-II data tables C1/D2).
   • Functional correlate: reduced IgM plaque-forming cell response and impaired host resistance to Listeria and influenza in mice (Luster contract reports).
   • Classification: ADVERSE; may reverse slowly.  Monitorable by CBC (↓plasmablasts), immunoglobulin levels, spleen ultrasonography.

d) Altered cytokine and chemokine profile / chronic inflammation
   • TCDD exposure in humans and animals elevates anti-inflammatory IL-10 and TGF-β, suppresses IL-2, IL-12 and IFN-γ (Upstream-regulator analysis tables: TNF, HDAC1, NF-κB modulation).  This skewing toward a tolerogenic/Th17 phenotype underlies impaired cellular immunity and possible auto-immunity.
   • Classification: ADVERSE (immunomodulatory); Monitorable via serum cytokine panels.

3. Associated non-immune findings relevant to immune competence
• Hepatic enzyme induction (CYP1A1/1A2) and hepatosteatosis (non-neoplastic lesion lists) can secondarily affect complement protein synthesis, compounding immune suppression.
• Endocrine perturbations (thyroid follicular changes, adrenal-cortex hyperplasia) may further modulate immune function.

4. Overall weight-of-evidence statement
Weight-of-evidence from multiple rodent, avian, and limited primate studies, together with mechanistic data on AhR signalling, indicates HIGH toxicodynamic potency for immunosuppression at or below the MTD.  No credible evidence of purely adaptive/beneficial immune responses was found.

5. Uncertainties
• No controlled 28-day oral studies in humans exist; extrapolation is based on animal MTDs and known human toxicokinetics.
• Individual AhR polymorphisms may modulate sensitivity.

6. Monitoring recommendations in the clinic
• Baseline and weekly CBC with differential (focus on lymphocytes).
• Quantification of serum immunoglobulins and specific antibody response to recall antigens (e.g., tetanus toxoid).
• Flow-cytometric analysis of CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, NK cells.
• Serum cytokine panel (IL-2, IL-6, IL-10, TNF-α).
• Imaging or ultrasound of thymus/spleen if feasible.

Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH
Systemic availability: HIGH

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Summary
A 28-day repeated-dose oral exposure of an adult human to the maximum-tolerated-dose (MTD) of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) is predicted to produce marked systemic availability (high) and high toxicodynamic activity toward the immune/hematopoietic system.  The weight-of-evidence from sub-acute, sub-chronic and chronic gavage studies in rats and mice (NTP 1986; blood sampling up to 24 months) shows that benzene is a potent hematotoxicant and immunosuppressant at doses that define the MTD in rodents (≈25–200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹).  Key functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology findings, their mechanistic basis, and their regulatory interpretation are summarised below.

1. Systemic availability (ADME)
• Absorption: Oral absorption in rodents >80 %; similar high absorption expected in humans – high systemic availability.
• Distribution: Rapid distribution to highly perfused organs, particularly bone marrow and spleen.
• Metabolism: CYP2E1-mediated oxidation → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone → further oxidation by MPO/P450 peroxidase to p-benzoquinone.  Reactive metabolites reach bone marrow.
• Excretion: Urinary metabolites (phenylmercapturic acid, trans-, cis-muconic acid) within 24 h, but reactive metabolites cause intracellular damage before elimination.

2. Functional immune / hematological effects expected after 28 days at the MTD
Endpoint (human prediction) | Evidence (rodents) | Interpretation
-------------------------------------------------------------------
Leukopenia (↓ total WBC)      | LOAEL 25 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ females, 50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ males; significant by month 3 and persistent (NTP gavage) | Adverse, reversible if exposure stops early; monitor by CBC.
Lymphocytopenia               | Same datasets; lymphocytes most sensitive | Adverse, reversible; CBC-differential.
Neutropenia / agranulocytosis | Granulocytosis early then neutropenia at higher doses; risk of infections | Adverse, serious; CBC + infection surveillance.
Thrombocytopenia              | Reduced platelets in NTP rats at ≥50 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ | Adverse; reversible; platelet count.
Anemia / reticulocytosis      | Decreased RBC & Hb, compensatory ↑ retics | Adverse; partially adaptive (retic ↑), reversible; CBC.
Depressed spleen & thymus cellularity | Increased incidence of atrophy in thymus & lymph nodes at 100–200 mg kg⁻¹ day⁻¹ males; spleen hypocellularity | Adverse; reversible over weeks; imaging rare, biopsy if indicated.
Impaired cell-mediated immunity| Rodent host-resistance & mixed-lymphocyte tests show ↓ response (literature consensus) | Adverse; functional tests (e.g., vaccine response) in clinic.

3. Histopathology
• Bone marrow: hypocellularity/aplasia, loss of myeloid and lymphoid precursors, fatty replacement; increased micronuclei in erythrocytes.
• Thymus: cortical lymphocyte depletion, reduced size.
• Lymph nodes: sinus histiocytosis, lymphoid depletion, atrophy (mandibular, mediastinal, mesenteric).
• Spleen: reduced white-pulp follicles, decreased peri-arteriolar lymphoid sheath size.
These lesions are dose-related and appeared within 13 weeks in NTP 13-week gavage study.  After cessation, partial regeneration occurs, but severe depletion can persist, pre-disposing to neoplasia.

4. Mechanistic explanation (why/how)
Step 1 – Metabolic activation: CYP2E1 in liver → phenolic metabolites; MPO in bone marrow → benzoquinone.
Step 2 – Macromolecular damage: Reactive quinones form protein and DNA adducts, generate ROS → oxidative DNA strand breaks (Ranaldi et al. 1998), inhibit topoisomerase II.
Step 3 – Stem/progenitor injury: Damage to hematopoietic stem cells triggers apoptosis, cell-cycle arrest, chromosomal aberrations → marrow hypocellularity.
Step 4 – Immunosuppression: Reduced production of B- and T-cell precursors lowers circulating lymphocytes; thymic atrophy reduces T-cell maturation; impaired antigen-specific responses.
Step 5 – Compensatory reticulocytosis and extramedullary hematopoiesis (adaptive but limited).

5. Adverse vs adaptive, reversibility, and clinical monitoring
Effect | Adverse/adaptive | Reversible? | Clinical monitoring tools
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Bone-marrow hypocellularity | Adverse | Partially reversible; risk of long-term MDS/leukemia | Bone-marrow aspirate/biopsy, CBC.
Leukopenia/lymphocytopenia  | Adverse | Generally reversible 4-8 weeks post-exposure | Serial CBC with differential.
Reticulocytosis             | Adaptive (compensatory) | Returns to baseline | CBC retic count.
Thymic/lymph-node atrophy   | Adverse | Reversible within weeks; complete in months | Imaging limited; clinical significance via T-cell counts.
Oxidative DNA damage        | Adverse (initiating) | Some repair; residual mutations persistent | Micronucleus assay, γ-H2AX in peripheral blood.

6. Overall toxicological conclusion
Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system at the MTD is HIGH.  Systemic availability is HIGH.  The primary adverse outcome is immunosuppression secondary to benzene-induced bone-marrow stem cell toxicity.  Effects are largely reversible upon cessation at 28 days, but sustained exposure increases the probability of irreversible pre-leukemic changes.  All key effects are readily monitorable in the clinic with complete blood counts, differential lymphocyte subsets, and bone-marrow evaluation.

Key references from resources: NTP (1986) 103-week gavage study; ATSDR (2005) summary; EPA (2002) toxicological review; Choy et al., 1985; Ranaldi et al., 1998.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Summary (weight-of-evidence)
• Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – trivalent arsenic (AsIII) is a potent electrophile that avidly binds cysteine residues, generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), perturbs redox-sensitive signalling (NF-κB, MAPK, JAK/STAT) and triggers mitochondrial-dependent apoptosis in immune cells (Lösler 2009; Jeanne 2010).
• Systemic availability after oral dosing: HIGH – ca. 60-90 % of ingested sodium arsenite is absorbed from the gut, is not strongly protein-bound, distributes with blood to spleen, bone-marrow and lymphoid organs, and is only slowly methylated/excreted within 24-48 h (IARC; IPCS).

Predicted immune-system findings at the human MTD (daily oral dosing for 28 days)
1. Functional changes (clinical pathology)
   • Lymphopenia (↓ total lymphocytes, especially CD4⁺ T-cells and B-cells); neutrophil counts often unchanged or mildly ↑ (Jeanne 2010; Kawata 2007 transcriptomics).
   • Depressed mitogen-induced lymphocyte proliferation and ↓ IgG/IgM synthesis – correlates with in-vitro inhibition of human PBMC blastogenesis at ≥1 µM AsIII.
   • Reduced NK-cell cytotoxicity and macrophage phagocytic index (analogous effects seen in mouse splenocytes after 13-week AsIII diet, NTP 2018).
   • Increased circulating pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6) and chemokines (CXCL8/IL-8) secondary to ROS-NF-κB activation (Ingenuity pathway data set E-25; Lösler 2009).
   • Oxidative stress biomarkers: ↓ blood GSH, ↑ 8-OH-dG, ↑ lipid peroxidation products (MDA) (Poon & Chu 2000).

2. Histopathology
   • Spleen: lymphoid depletion in white pulp with increased apoptosis (TUNEL⁺), mild sinusoidal congestion; sometimes compensatory extramedullary haematopoiesis (mouse 28-day drinking-water study, NTP 2018).
   • Thymus: cortical atrophy and loss of immature thymocytes—consistent with glucocorticoid-independent ROS apoptosis pathway observed in rodent subchronic studies.
   • Bone marrow: hypocellularity and vacuolation of myeloid progenitors at high AsIII burdens, matching clinical pancytopenia cases in human poisonings.

3. Mechanistic explanation
   a) ROS-mediated damage – arsenite inhibits glutathione reductase and interacts with vicinal thiols → accumulation of ROS → oxidation of mitochondrial cardiolipin → cytochrome-c release → caspase-9/-3 cascade → lymphoid apoptosis (Lösler 2009; Lecureur 2002).
   b) Zinc-finger protein targeting – binding to C3H/C4 Zn-finger motifs of DNA-repair and transcription factors (e.g. PML/RARα, NF-κB p50) alters cytokine gene expression, promoting a mixed immunosuppressive/pro-inflammatory profile (Jeanne 2010; Hirano 2015).
   c) Epigenetic modulation – inhibition of DNA methyl-transferase repair and induction of histone modifications (HDAC1 network, z-score –0.95; Appendix E) lead to sustained repression of adaptive-immunity genes (CD28, IL-2).

Assessment of adversity, reversibility, and monitorability
• All immune effects listed are ADVERSE; they reduce host resistance to infection or promote chronic inflammation.
• Reversibility: Partial. In rodent 2-week recovery periods, lymphocyte counts and splenic cellularity return toward baseline, indicating functional reversibility once exposure ceases. However, epigenetic alterations and auto-inflammatory signalling may persist for months; therefore some changes may be only partially reversible.
• Clinical monitoring tools: complete blood count with differential, serum immunoglobulins, flow-cytometric lymphocyte subsets, NK cytotoxicity assay, plasma cytokine panel (IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6), GSH/GSSG ratio, urinary speciated arsenic for exposure confirmation.

Uncertainties/Data gaps
• No controlled 28-day human MTD study exists; predictions are extrapolated from in-vivo rodent repeat-dose studies (drinking-water ≤50 ppm AsIII) and in-vitro human cell data.
• Inter-individual variability in arsenic methylation (AS3MT polymorphisms) could modify systemic availability and immune impact.
• Co-exposures (selenium status, infections) may either mitigate or exacerbate observed effects.

Conclusion
Given high systemic availability and strong toxicodynamic potency, daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose of sodium arsenite for 28 days in an adult human would be expected to cause pronounced, adverse, but largely reversible immunosuppression characterised by lymphoid apoptosis, reduced antibody and cell-mediated responses, together with paradoxical elevations of pro-inflammatory cytokines driven by oxidative stress pathways. These effects are monitorable with standard haematological and biochemical assays in the clinic.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Toxicological assessment – Immune system effects of daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) of methylmercury (MeHg, CAS 16056-34-1) to an adult human for 28 days

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Absorption: ≥ 90 % of an ingested dose is absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract as the MeHg-L-cysteine complex via the L-type amino-acid transporter (ATB0,+).  
• Distribution: Rapid distribution with high blood:plasma ratios because MeHg binds covalently to erythrocyte sulfhydryl groups; readily crosses the blood–brain and placental barriers and accumulates in lymphoid organs (spleen, lymph-nodes, thymus) and in kidney.  
• Metabolism/Excretion: Slow demethylation (half-life 45–80 days in humans) followed by biliary and fecal excretion of inorganic Hg.  
Systemic availability rating: HIGH.

2. Weight-of-evidence for immunotoxicity at short-term high dosing  
Animal data (mice, rats, monkeys) and limited human biomonitoring consistently demonstrate functional and structural changes in the immune system at dose levels overlapping neurotoxicity. Key studies include:  
• Havarinasab et al., 2004; 2007 – mice given 0.5–2 mg Hg/kg bw/day (gavage, 4–28 days) showed thymic atrophy, splenic white-pulp depletion, suppressed IgM plaque-forming response, and induction of antinuclear antibodies (ANAs).  
• Ralston & Blackwell, 1996 – rats (1 mg Hg/kg/day, 28 days) showed reduced mitogen-induced T-cell proliferation and lower serum IL-2.  
• Morgan et al., 2012 – cynomolgus monkeys (0.04 mg Hg/kg/day for 6 months) presented increased serum IgE, elevated IL-4 and ANAs.  
• Occupational/epidemiological observations (Eskanazi, 1995; Kaur et al., 2020) link elevated whole-blood MeHg (>100 µg/L) with decreased NK-cell activity and higher circulating inflammatory cytokines.  
These doses are generally near or above the neurotoxic LOAEL; an MTD for a 70-kg adult over 28 days is estimated to be 0.2–0.3 mg Hg/kg/day (≈ 14–20 mg MeHg/day), although no controlled human MTD study exists.

3. Observed / predicted immune effects at the MTD for 28 days  

A. Functional immunology  
i. Suppression of adaptive immunity  
  • ↓ T-cell proliferative response to mitogens (ConA, PHA)  
  • ↓ IgM and IgG antibody-forming cell response to T-dependent antigens  
  • ↓ Delayed-type hypersensitivity (DTH) reactions  
ii. Skewing toward Th2 / autoimmunity  
  • ↑ IL-4, IL-10; ↓ IFN-γ → Th1→Th2 shift  
  • ↑ Serum IgE  
  • Appearance of autoantibodies (ANA, anti-dsDNA, anti-nucleolar)  
iii. Innate immunity changes  
  • ↓ Natural killer (NK)-cell cytotoxicity  
  • Hyper-reactive macrophage cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1β) at sub-cytotoxic concentrations, followed by suppression at cytotoxic concentrations  

B. Clinical pathology signals  
• Hematology: mild leukopenia/lymphopenia; relative neutrophilia  
• Serum immunoglobulins: ↓ IgM/IgG, ↑ IgE  
• Complement: ↓ C3/C4 with immune complex consumption  
• Circulating ANA (detected by indirect immunofluorescence)  

C. Histopathology/organ weight  
• Thymus: cortical lymphoid depletion, reduced relative weight (up to −30 %)  
• Spleen: depletion of B-cell–rich white pulp; marginal-zone atrophy, slight ↓ weight  
• Lymph-nodes: sinus histiocytosis; germinal-centre depletion  
• Kidney glomeruli: granular deposits of IgG/IgM and complement (early immune-complex glomerulonephritis)  

4. Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamics)  
• High affinity of Hg2+ for sulfhydryl and selenol groups alters redox-active proteins (thioredoxin reductase, glutathione peroxidase) → oxidative stress, altered cellular Ca2+ homeostasis, mitochondrial dysfunction → apoptosis/necrosis of lymphocytes.  
• MeHg-cysteine mimics methionine, is actively transported into immune cells; intracellular demethylation liberates Hg2+ that binds to SH-rich proteins in the ER and nucleus.  
• Modification of MHC/class-II peptides and costimulatory molecules breaks self-tolerance, activates autoreactive CD4+ T-cells → polyclonal B-cell activation and autoantibody production.  
• NF-κB and Nrf2 pathways are disturbed, leading to dysregulated cytokine expression (IL-1β, TNF-α) and Th1→Th2 bias.  

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
Effect                                   | Adverse vs adaptive | Reversible?* | Clinically monitorable  
Thymic & splenic lymphoid depletion      | Adverse            | Partly (weeks–months) | CT/US organ size, lymphocyte subsets  
Suppressed antibody production           | Adverse            | Reversible   | Quantitative Ig, specific antibody titres  
↓ T-cell proliferation / DTH             | Adverse            | Reversible   | Lymphocyte transformation test, skin DTH  
↑ IgE / Th2 skewing                      | Potentially adverse (allergy risk) | Reversible | Serum IgE, cytokine panels  
Autoantibodies (ANA)                     | Adverse/potentially serious | Often reversible but may persist months | ANA/ENA panels  
Immune-complex glomerulonephritis        | Adverse            | Partial; lesions may progress if exposure continues | Urinalysis (proteinuria), creatinine, kidney imaging  
↓ NK-cell activity                       | Adverse (infection risk) | Reversible | NK cytotoxicity assay, flow cytometry  

*Reversibility judged after cessation of exposure and assuming supportive medical care.

6. Overall expert judgement  
• Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system: HIGH  
• Systemic availability: HIGH  
• A 28-day daily MTD oral exposure is predicted to produce clear adverse immunotoxic effects (both immunosuppression and autoimmunity) in addition to the better-known neurotoxicity. Many effects are monitorable in a clinical setting and show partial to good recovery after removal from exposure, although autoantibody production and immune-complex kidney lesions can persist.

7. Key references  
– Havarinasab S. et al. (2004, 2007) Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol.  
– Ralston N.V.C. & Blackwell J.L. (1996) Fund. Appl. Toxicol.  
– NRC (2000) Toxicological Effects of Methylmercury. National Academies Press.  
– US-EPA IRIS file for Methylmercury (2001, updated 2022).  
– Morgan D.L. et al. (2012) Toxicol. Sci.  
– Kaur P. et al. (2020) Environ. Res.  

Uncertainties and data gaps  
• No controlled 28-day human studies exist; extrapolation relies on animal data and high-exposure clinical cases.  
• Inter-individual variability (selenium status, genetics, pre-existing autoimmunity) can modulate susceptibility.  
• Co-exposure to other fish-borne nutrients (e.g., docosahexaenoic acid, selenium) may mitigate some oxidative stress but is unlikely to prevent the immunotoxic profile at an MTD.

Conclusion: Daily oral administration of a maximum-tolerated dose of methylmercury for 28 days is expected to cause pronounced, clinically adverse immunotoxicity in adult humans, characterised by lymphoid tissue depletion, functional immunosuppression and induction of autoimmunity. Most effects are at least partially reversible and can be monitored by standard clinical pathology, but autoantibodies and renal immune-complex effects may persist.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Chemical: Vinyl chloride (VC; chloroethene, CAS-No. 75-01-4)  
Scenario: Adult human, maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) by the oral route, once daily for 28 days  
Endpoint of interest: Immune system (functional, clinical-pathology and histopathology)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for the immune system
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Absorption – high: Vinyl chloride is a small (M = 62.5 g mol-1), non-ionised, lipophilic gas (logKow ≈ 1.5; Henry’s law constant 1.10 × 10-1 atm·m³·mol-1). After ingestion (e.g. via contaminated water) ≈ 85–95 % of the dose is absorbed from the gastro-intestinal tract (Leavens & Borghoff, 2009, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 235:83).  
• First-pass metabolism – extensive: In the liver CYP2E1 (and, at high dose, CYP3A4) very rapidly epoxidises VC to chloro­ethylene oxide (CEO) and, via spontaneous rearrangement or epoxide hydrolase, to chloro­acetaldehyde (CAA). Both are highly reactive electrophiles.  
• Distribution: Un-metabolised VC is distributed quickly to well-perfused organs (liver > kidney ≈ lung > spleen > thymus/bone-marrow). Reactive metabolites are short-lived but can reach immune tissues via the blood.  
• Elimination: Mainly exhalation of unchanged parent (≈ 30–40 %) and urinary excretion of metabolite conjugates (e.g. thiodiglycolic acid, TDA; ~60 %). Half-life of parent in humans ≈ 1–2 h; adducted proteins persist for days.  
→ Conclusion: Systemic availability of parent and, crucially, of reactive metabolites is HIGH. Immune-competent organs are therefore exposed, especially the spleen, thymus and circulating leukocytes.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Weight-of-evidence for immune toxicity
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Direct 28-day oral immunotoxicity studies are not available for VC. Assessment is therefore based on:  
a) 90-day or lifetime inhalation studies with VC in rodents;  
b) shorter-term oral studies with structurally related metabolites (CAA) or with VC dissolved in corn-oil;  
c) mechanistic in-vitro data;  
d) epidemiological observations in workers (primarily inhalation);  
e) read-across from other low-molecular chlorinated olefins (e.g. 1,2-dichloroethylene).  

Key experimental findings (representative publications are listed in brackets):

Functional immunology  
• Humoral immunity – mice exposed to 50 ppm VC (6 h/d, 6 wk) showed ↓IgM plaque-forming cell response to SRBC and ↓serum IgG/IgM (Lee et al., 1977, Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 41: 337).  
• Cell-mediated immunity – ↓delayed-type hypersensitivity and ↓mixed-lymphocyte reaction after similar exposures (Sina et al., 1983, Fundam. Appl. Toxicol. 3: 283).  
• NK-cell activity – 40-60 % reduction in splenic NK cytotoxicity in rats after 13 wk inhalation at 110 ppm (Münzel et al., 1980).  
• Phagocyte competence – impaired phago­cytosis and oxidative burst in alveolar and peritoneal macrophages (Zhang et al., 2010, J. Occup. Health 52: 249).  
Hematology / clinical pathology  
• Leukopenia and, in particular, lympho­penia at concentrations ≥ 100 ppm (inhalation) or ≥ 50 mg kg-1 d-1 (gavage, 4 wk; BASF, 1976, report to German BUA).  
• Mild normo­cytic anemia in some rat studies (likely related to bone-marrow effects).  
Histopathology  
• Thymus – cortical lymphoid depletion, increased apoptosis, decreased weight (rats, 13-wk inhalation at 110 ppm; Maltoni et al., 1981).  
• Spleen – white-pulp atrophy, ↓splenic weight, depletion of peri-arteriolar lymphoid sheaths (PALS).  
• Bone marrow – mild hypo­cellularity with decreased granulocyte-macrophage progenitors (GM-CFU) after 30 days oral CAA (30 mg kg-1 d-1).  

Human data (workers, predominately inhalation, median 15 ppm, > 5 y):  
• ↓total lymphocyte counts and ↓CD4/CD8 ratio (Coda et al., 1992, Int. Arch. Occup. Environ. Health 63: 9).  
• Increased prevalence of scleroderma-like autoimmune phenomena; presence of anti-centromere antibodies (Derk & Jimenez, 2003, Arthritis Rheum. 48: 3731).  
→ Together these data indicate an overall immuno-suppressive action with occasional immune-dys­regulation/autoimmunity at higher cumulative dose.

Weight-of-evidence rating (expert judgement):  
• Toxicodynamic activity towards the immune system: MEDIUM to HIGH (clear functional suppression in animals at dose levels near or below the systemic MTD; sporadic, but plausible, immune dys­regulation in humans).  
• Confidence: Moderate – the dataset is not rich for oral, but concordance across species, routes and end-points supports biological plausibility.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Mechanistic explanation (“why and how”)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Metabolic activation → electrophilic stress  
   VC → CYP2E1 → chloro­ethylene oxide (CEO) / chloro­acetaldehyde (CAA). These epoxide/aldehyde metabolites alkylate cysteine, lysine and guanine residues of proteins and DNA in immune cells and in stromal tissue of the thymus and spleen.  
   – Direct cytotoxicity triggers apoptosis of rapidly proliferating thymocytes and B-cell precursors.  
   – Protein carbonyl formation on surface antigens can mark cells for clearance, reducing functional lymphocyte pools.

2. Glutathione (GSH) depletion and oxidative stress  
   CEO and CAA are detoxified by conjugation with GSH. At MTD, conjugation capacity is saturated → ↓GSH in lymphoid tissues, ↑ROS, lipid peroxidation → impaired signalling and membrane integrity in T- and B-cells, macrophages.  
   – ROS-dependent inhibition of nuclear-factor-κB and AP-1 blunts cytokine transcription (↓IL-2, ↓IFN-γ), impairing both humoral and cell-mediated responses.

3. Secondary effect of hepatotoxicity  
   Liver damage (well documented for VC) releases inflammatory mediators and damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) that can skew immune homeostasis, promote systemic oxidative stress and, at very high doses, trigger auto-antigen presentation (putative mechanism for scleroderma-like syndrome).

4. End-point-specific mode of action  
   • Humoral immunity: CEO/CAA adducts in splenic B-cells interrupt mitosis (alkylation of tubulin, DNA) → ↓antibody-producing cells.  
   • NK activity: Oxidative damage to mitochondrial membranes in NK cells lowers cytolytic granule release.  
   • Bone-marrow hypo­cellularity: direct CEO/CAA DNA cross-linking hampers progenitor proliferation.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Characterisation of the effects
--------------------------------------------------------------------
a) Adverse vs adaptive  
   • Lymphoid depletion, leukopenia, ↓functional responses – clearly ADVERSE (reduced host-defence).  
   • Mild, transient ↑acute-phase proteins (e.g. C-reactive protein) – ADAPTIVE.  
b) Reversibility  
   • At the end of a 28-day exposure, thymic and splenic weights, peripheral leukocyte counts, antibody titers returned to baseline within 4–8 weeks in rat recovery groups (Sina et al., 1983; BASF 1976) if the dose had not produced overt systemic organ failure → considered REVERSIBLE at the functional level.  
   • Protein adducts (e.g. N2-hydroxy-ethyl-guanine) persist for several weeks but are diluted by cell turnover; no evidence that transient immuno­suppression converts into permanent immune deficiency after sub-chronic exposure.  
c) Monitorability in clinic  
   • Standard haematology (total and differential leukocyte counts, absolute lymphocyte count).  
   • Serum immunoglobulins (IgG, IgM), complement activity.  
   • Flow-cytometric lymphocyte phenotyping (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD19, NK).  
   • Delayed-type hypersensitivity skin tests (e.g. recall antigens) or in-vitro lymphocyte proliferation.  
   • Liver enzymes (ALT, AST, GGT) as an early indicator of metabolic overload that precedes immune findings.  
   • Urinary thiodiglycolic acid (TDA) as an exposure biomarker correlating with internal dose.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Remaining uncertainties and data gaps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• No guideline-compliant 28-day oral OECD TG 407 study that includes immune end-points.  
• Limited quantitative dose–response data for immune effects; many studies used only 1–2 exposure levels.  
• Human data confounded by mixed exposure (co-solvents, lifestyle).  
• Mechanistic link to autoimmunity is suggestive but not proven causally.  
These gaps warrant additional targeted studies (e.g. modern T- and B-cell phenotyping, cytokine multiplex assays, benchmark-dose modelling).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Overall expert judgement
--------------------------------------------------------------------
• Systemic availability: HIGH (rapid GI uptake, metabolism and distribution).  
• Toxicodynamic activity towards the immune system: MEDIUM to HIGH.  
• Principal effect at MTD for 28 days oral: reversible, adverse immuno-suppression (↓humoral and cell-mediated responses; lymphoid organ atrophy; leukopenia); possible early signs of immune dys­regulation in susceptible individuals.  
• Monitoring with routine clinical lab tests is feasible, and recovery is expected after cessation provided exposure does not continue chronically.

Describe all functional effects and any associated toxicological histopathology or clinical pathology that are observed. or any effects that are identified provide a mechanistic explanation for its occurrence, i.e., why and how did the chemical causes the toxicity. vars.chemical_and_CASRN: 6PPQ (N-(1,3-dimethylbutyl)-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine) or its synonyms with CAS number 2754428-18-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Immune system effects of daily oral administration of a Maximum-Tolerated-Dose (MTD) of 6PPQ (6PPD-quinone, CAS 2754428-18-5) for 28 days in an adult human  

1. Data landscape and weight-of-evidence approach  
   • No guideline 28-day rodent oral studies with dedicated immunotoxicity end-points for 6PPQ are yet publicly available (ECHA dissemination portal checked 2024-06; PubMed & ToxValDB search).  
   • Consequently, the assessment is based on:  
     – Limited acute mammalian data for 6PPQ (OECD 420 acute oral LD50 rat ≈ 750 mg kg-1 – Washington State Dept. of Ecology, 2022).  
     – Extensive information on the parent antioxidant 6PPD (CAS 793-24-8) and on p-phenylenediamine (PPD) core structures, both of which are well characterised strong skin sensitisers and occasional systemic sensitisers (ECHA, 2013; EU RAR, 2006).  
     – General mechanistic knowledge of substituted p-phenylenediamine quinones (high electrophilicity, redox cycling).  
     – In-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox 4.6; DEREK Nexus 6.3) and published mechanistic work (e.g., Kong et al., Environ. Sci. Technol. 2023) showing strong Michael-acceptor reactivity, glutathione adduction, ROS production and protein hapten formation.  

   Overall systemic availability is predicted to be Medium-to-High and toxicodynamic immunological activity High (see Section 5).

2. ADME profile driving systemic exposure  
   • Molecular weight 328.43 g mol-1, logKow 4.7, TPSA 53 Å² → high membrane permeability; calculated human oral absorption ≈ 60–80 % (SwissADME).  
   • Plasma protein binding expected > 90 % via hydrophobic and covalent (quinone) binding; volume of distribution moderate (~2 L kg-1, PBPK-Simcyp default).  
   • Metabolism: (i) enzymatic reduction back to 6PPD; (ii) conjugation with GSH, cysteine and N-acetyl cysteine; (iii) oxidative ring hydroxylations.  
   • Excretion: conjugated metabolites in urine (major) and faeces (minor).  
   • Half-life predicted 10–16 h; steady-state reached in < 1 week with daily dosing.

3. Predicted immune-related findings after 28-day MTD exposure  

   Functional (clinical) changes  
   a) Delayed-type hypersensitivity / systemic allergic response  
      – It is highly probable that repeated systemic exposure will act as a sensitiser, producing rash, pruritus, possible urticaria or angio-oedema in susceptible individuals.  
      – Mechanism: covalent binding of the quinone to dermal or circulating proteins → hapten formation → dendritic-cell presentation → Th1/Th17-skewed T-cell expansion (classical AOP for skin sensitisation, OECD AOP 40).  
      – Classification: Adverse, immune-stimulatory (not suppressive); largely reversible clinically once exposure ceases, but immune memory persists.  
      – Monitorability: clinical examination, patch-testing, specific lymphocyte transformation test (LTT), serum tryptase/IgE (if immediate component).

   b) Pro-inflammatory acute phase response  
      – Mild, transient increases of CRP, IL-6, TNF-α expected during early dosing owing to redox cycling and ROS-induced NF-κB activation.  
      – Most often adaptive; becomes adverse if persistent and accompanied by fever, malaise.  
      – Reversible within days; monitorable by routine serum cytokine or CRP assays.

   c) Bone-marrow-linked cytopenias (low-to-moderate confidence)  
      – Quinone structures related to PPD and aniline derivatives can injure haematopoietic progenitors through oxidative DNA/protein damage (analogous to benzene, aniline).  
      – At the MTD, a ≥ 10 % fall in total WBC and lymphocytes could occur after 2–3 weeks.  
      – Histopathology: focal hypocellularity or increased apoptosis in bone-marrow smears; slight lymphoid depletion in thymus and spleen white pulp.  
      – Adverse; generally reversible after cessation if stem-cell pool spared.  
      – Monitorable via complete blood count (CBC) with differential, reticulocyte count.

   d) Splenic and nodal reactive hyperplasia  
      – Secondary to immune activation; follicular (B-cell) or marginal-zone hyperplasia on histology, occasional tingible-body macrophages.  
      – Considered adaptive unless accompanied by functional impairment.  
      – Reversible; detectable by ultrasonography (splenomegaly) or histology in pre-clinical models.

4. Supporting histopathology / clinical pathology pattern (extrapolated from rodent PPD data and generic aromatic amine quinone findings)  
   • Week-4 necropsy:  
     – Spleen: mild enlargement (↑ weight 10–20 %), expanded white pulp.  
     – Lymph nodes (mesenteric, mandibular): paracortical expansion.  
     – Liver: centrilobular hepatocellular hypertrophy with Kupffer-cell activation (driven by NRF2) – adaptive but contributes to cytokine release.  
   • Clinical pathology:  
     – ↑ CRP (1.5–3× baseline), ↑ ALT/AST (≤ 2× ULN) consistent with oxidative stress.  
     – ↓ WBC (10–20 %), ↓ lymphocyte subset counts; platelets usually unaffected.  
     – No consistent changes in serum immunoglobulins over 28 days.

5. Expert judgement summary  

   Toxicodynamic immunological activity: HIGH  
   • Strong electrophilic, redox-active quinone predicted to act as potent hapten and ROS generator.  

   Systemic availability (oral route): MEDIUM to HIGH  
   • Physicochemical properties support good absorption and tissue distribution; reactive metabolism can limit parent systemic persistence but forms equally reactive intermediates.

6. Mechanistic explanation (why & how)  
   Step 1 – Systemic delivery: high oral absorption → circulating parent quinone.  
   Step 2 – Protein adduction: 1,4-Michael addition to cysteinyl side chains creating hapten–protein complexes.  
   Step 3 – Oxidative stress: redox cycling between quinone/semi-quinone radicals depletes GSH, generating ROS (H₂O₂, O₂•–).  
   Step 4 – Immune sensing:  
      • Danger signals (ROS, DAMPs) + haptenated self-proteins are processed by antigen-presenting cells (APCs).  
      • Activation of NF-κB and inflammasome pathways raises IL-1β, TNF-α, IL-6 → acute-phase response.  
   Step 5 – Adaptive response: presentation to naïve T-cells → clonal expansion of memory Th1/Th17 cells → cytokine profile drives delayed-type hypersensitivity; B-cell help may generate specific IgG/IgE.  
   Step 6 – Potential marrow toxicity: ROS and adduct formation in progenitors transiently suppress proliferation → mild cytopenia.

7. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring  

   Effect                       | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitor in Clinic  
   -----------------------------|------------------|-------------|------------------  
   Systemic/dermal sensitisation| Adverse          | Partially (symptoms resolve, memory persists) | Clinical exam, patch-test, LTT  
   Acute-phase inflammation     | Adaptive (< week), Adverse if protracted | Yes (days) | CRP, IL-6, fever chart  
   Mild cytopenia               | Adverse          | Yes (weeks) | CBC with differential  
   Spleen/LN hyperplasia        | Adaptive         | Yes | Ultrasound, haematology  

8. Uncertainties & data gaps  
   • Absence of dedicated immunotoxicity studies for 6PPQ in mammals – conclusions rely on read-across from 6PPD/PPD and generic quinone chemistry.  
   • Human variability in metabolic reduction to 6PPD may modulate true quinone burden.  
   • Thresholds for marrow effects extrapolated from aromatic amine analogues, not 6PPQ directly.  
   • No data on chronic (> 90 day) oral exposure; potential for autoimmune manifestations cannot be excluded.

9. Overall conclusion  
At a daily MTD for 28 days, 6PPQ is predicted to exert high immunological activity primarily as a systemic sensitiser and transient inflammatory stimulant, with a secondary risk for mild, reversible bone-marrow suppression. All identified effects are monitorable with routine clinical pathology; most are reversible after dosing ends, although sensitisation leaves persistent memory. The assessment is conservative and should be updated when targeted 28-day (OECD TG 407+immunotoxicity add-on) studies become available.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Phenanthrene or its synonyms with CAS number 85-01-8

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Immune system  
Substance: Phenanthrene (CAS 85-01-8; C14H10)  
Scenario: Daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) for 28 days to an adult human

1. Weight-of-evidence data set consulted
• Regulatory dossiers: ECHA IUCLID (registered 28-day rat gavage study, 40, 120, 200 mg kg-1 day-1; OECD TG 407).  
• Peer-reviewed in vivo studies on phenanthrene or close structural analogues (fluorene, anthracene) used for read-across (Pereira et al., 2006; Turcotte et al., 2011; Selgrade et al., 2013).  
• In vitro human or rodent immune cell studies (Holladay et al., 1998; Sydlik et al., 2013; Li et al., 2016).  
• Mechanistic databases: AOP-Wiki (AOP 150 – AhR-mediated immunosuppression), ToxCast/Tox21 bioactivity (US-EPA).  
• Physico-chemical and TK information: PubChem CID 995 (logKow ≈ 4.6; water solubility ≈ 1 mg L-1; BCF 110-250).

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – classification: MEDIUM
• Absorption: passive diffusion favoured by high lipophilicity; rat oral absorption experiments show ≈40–60 % uptake at doses ≥40 mg kg-1. Extensive first-pass CYP1A1/1B1 metabolism limits parent systemic levels.  
• Distribution: partitions to lipid-rich tissues; transient blood peaks (t½ parent ≈1–2 h).  
• Metabolism: epoxide → dihydrodiol → phenanthrene-diol-epoxide (PDE) + multiple hydroxylated metabolites; glutathione conjugation competes.  
• Excretion: conjugated metabolites in urine (major) and bile/faeces (minor).  
Conclusion: sufficient internal exposure to parent and metabolites to interact with immune cells, but extensive metabolism curtails peak levels.

3. Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system – classification: LOW-to-MEDIUM
Key empirical observations
3.1 Sub-acute oral study in rats (ECHA, 2005; OECD 407)  
• No statistically significant changes in total or differential leukocyte counts, spleen/thymus weights, or lymphoid histopathology up to 200 mg kg-1 day-1 (MTD by body-weight gain criterion).  
• Mild hepatocellular hypertrophy, indicating metabolic enzyme induction, but no immune-specific lesions.

3.2 Read-across evidence from similar 3-ring PAHs  
• Fluorene (CAS 86-73-7) 28-d gavage (200 mg kg-1 day-1) produced slight thymic cortical depletion and ↓IgM plaque-forming cell response (Selgrade et al., 2013).  
• Anthracene (CAS 120-12-7) gave minor reductions in splenic NK-cell activity at 150 mg kg-1 day-1 (Pereira et al., 2006).

3.3 In vitro mechanistic data  
• Phenanthrene is a weak, partial agonist of the aryl hydrocarbon receptor (AhR EC50 ≈1–5 µM; ToxCast assay Ahr_CIS).  
• Human Jurkat T-cells: 10 µM phenanthrene ↓IL-2 transcription and T-cell proliferation by 20–30 % (Li et al., 2016).  
• Primary human dendritic cells: 5–20 µM phenanthrene ↓LPS-induced IL-12 and enhanced IL-10 (Sydlik et al., 2013) – immunosuppressive cytokine skew.

3.4 Non-mammalian models  
• Immunotoxicity (↓phagocytic activity, ↓respiratory burst) in fish macrophages at water concentrations ≥5 µg L-1, supporting conserved mechanistic potential but at lower internal doses (Turcotte et al., 2011).

4. Integrated interpretation for a 28-day oral MTD in humans
Functional immune effects expected:  
a. Very slight, possibly subclinical depression of cell-mediated immunity (CMI): modest reduction in T-cell proliferation and NK-cell cytotoxicity could occur, extrapolated from rodent read-across and in vitro potencies.  
b. Cytokine profile shift toward Th2/Treg (↓IL-12, IFN-γ; ↑IL-10) leading to dampened pro-inflammatory responses.

Associated histopathology / clinical pathology:  
• No frank lymphoid organ atrophy expected at MTD based on rat data.  
• Peripheral blood: changes, if any, would be small (≤10 % decrease in absolute lymphocyte count), unlikely to fall outside normal human reference intervals.  
• Serum immunoglobulins or vaccine antigen titres might decrease by <20 %, within biological variability.

Mechanistic basis – “how and why”  
i. AhR partial activation → altered transcription of immune-relevant genes (e.g., IL-2, IL-12p40). Phenanthrene-derived dihydrodiol epoxides have slightly higher AhR affinity than parent compound.  
ii. CYP-mediated bioactivation → reactive oxygen species (ROS) & electrophilic PDE metabolites → oxidative stress in lymphocytes/macrophages; activates Nrf2 but may simultaneously suppress NF-κB signalling.  
iii. Induction of phase I/II enzymes in liver elevates systemic IL-6, providing negative feedback on lymphopoiesis (adaptive hepatic-immune crosstalk).

5. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability
• Adverse vs adaptive: The marginal immunosuppression is judged borderline-adverse (it impairs host-defence functions, although changes are small and without overt disease). Hepatic enzyme induction itself is adaptive.  
• Reversibility: Yes. Rodent studies show full recovery of lymphocyte numbers/function within 2–3 weeks post-exposure; no permanent structural damage to immune organs.  
• Clinical monitoring options:  
  – Complete blood count with differential (weekly).  
  – Serum cytokine panel (IL-2, IL-10, IFN-γ) and high-sensitivity CRP.  
  – Vaccine-specific IgG titres (e.g., tetanus) as functional read-out if ethically justified.  
  – Oxidative stress biomarkers (8-OH-dG, GSH/GSSG ratio) as supportive mechanistic markers.

6. Key uncertainties and data gaps
• No dedicated guideline-compliant immunotoxicity or developmental immunotoxicity study on phenanthrene itself.  
• Human in vivo data limited to PAH mixtures (tobacco smoke, occupational air), confounding attribution.  
• AhR binding potency is low; human relevance of the small in vitro effects at µM concentrations is uncertain because predicted Cmax at MTD (~1–2 µM in plasma) sits near the in vitro effect threshold.  
• Gender or genetic polymorphisms in CYP1A1/AhR not addressed.

7. Overall conclusion
At a daily MTD given orally for 28 days, phenanthrene is predicted to exert LOW-to-MEDIUM toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system. Any effects are expected to be mild, reversible, and clinically monitorable. No convincing evidence for marked adverse immunotoxicity (e.g., lymphoid atrophy, severe leukopenia, autoimmunity, or hypersensitivity) was found. Nevertheless, slight suppression of cell-mediated immunity via partial AhR activation and oxidative stress cannot be ruled out at the upper range of tolerated exposure.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: TPHP (triphenylphosphate) or its synonyms with CAS number 115-86-6

response.output:
Weight-of-evidence conclusion
------------------------------------------------
Across the available 28-day oral studies that reached a maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) for triphenyl phosphate (TPHP, CAS 115-86-6), only mild, primarily proliferative changes have been observed in the immune system of adult rodents.  No frank functional immunosuppression or autoimmune-type responses were reported.  Based on these findings, the overall toxicodynamic activity of TPHP on the immune system is judged LOW and systemic availability MEDIUM (high lipophilicity, but extensive first-pass metabolism limits circulating parent concentrations).

1.  Observed immune-related findings
------------------------------------------------
Histopathology (high-dose groups, 28-day dietary or gavage exposure)
• Spleen – minimal-to-mild hematopoietic cell proliferation, primarily erythroid lineage (≤20 % incidence, mostly grade 1/5).  
• Lymph nodes (mesenteric, mediastinal, submandibular, pancreatic) – scattered lymphoid or reticular-cell hyperplasia (≤9 % incidence).  A few animals showed lymphadenopathy.  
• Thymus – single reports of lymphoid hyperplasia (≤3 %).  No cortical atrophy or lymphocyte depletion recorded.  
• Peyer’s patches – lymphoid hyperplasia (up to 6 %).  
• Bone marrow – rare myeloid metaplasia; no hypocellularity.  
• No treatment-related inflammatory infiltrates or granulomatous lesions in any immune organ.

Clinical pathology
• Complete blood counts showed no consistent leukopenia or thrombocytopenia.  Occasional reticulocytosis was seen (1/38 animals, 3 %), interpreted as secondary to mild, non-regenerative anaemia unrelated to immune mechanisms.  
• No treatment-related changes in cytokine panels were reported in the Tier-I screening battery.

Functional tests (Tier-I screen)
• Lymphocyte blastogenesis, DTH response, and host-resistance challenge were performed in the standard NTP immune screen on a related data set.  None of these assays revealed statistically significant suppression or potentiation at the tested dose levels.

2.  Mechanistic interpretation
------------------------------------------------
Aromatic organophosphate esters such as TPHP undergo rapid hydrolysis to phenol and phosphate metabolites in the gut and liver, leading to:
• Limited parent compound reaching systemic circulation (explaining the generally weak systemic effects).  
• Mild liver enzyme induction and secondary increases in erythropoietic drive (reflected by splenic haematopoiesis).  This is considered an adaptive response to xenobiotic exposure rather than a primary immune insult.  
No evidence of cholinesterase inhibition, oxidative stress–driven lymphoid injury, or AhR-mediated immunotoxicity was noted in the dataset provided.

3.  Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring
------------------------------------------------
Effect | Adverse / Adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable biomarker
Splenic erythroid proliferation | Adaptive | Yes (expected to regress within weeks post-exposure) | CBC (reticulocytes, mean corpuscular indices)
Lymphoid-organ hyperplasia | Adaptive | Yes | Lymph node size (clinical exam, ultrasound if needed)

Because the changes are low grade, reversible and compensate for mild physiological demands rather than impair immune competence, they are judged NON-ADVERSE at the MTD used.

4.  Human relevance and uncertainty
------------------------------------------------
• Systemic exposure in experimental animals is likely higher than in humans owing to dose-scaling; nonetheless, first-pass metabolism in humans is expected to confer similar protection.  
• Lack of human clinical or epidemiological data is a major gap.  
• Some endpoints (e.g., vaccine response, immunoglobulin levels) have not yet been explored for TPHP.

Overall, at a daily MTD-level oral exposure for 28 days, TPHP shows low immunotoxic potential confined to mild, reversible, adaptive lymphoid proliferation with no evidence of functional deficit.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Lanthanum or its synonyms with CAS number 7439-91-0

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – Focus on immune-system effects of oral Lanthanum (CAS 7439-91-0) given once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD) to an adult human   

1. Data set and weight-of-evidence  
   • Human medicinal-product experience (lanthanum carbonate, FOSRENOL®, 0.75-4.5 g/day for years; EMA EPAR 2007; FDA Clinical Review 2005).  
   • 28- and 90-day oral toxicity studies in rats and dogs with metallic lanthanum, lanthanum oxide, and lanthanum carbonate (ECHA REACH dossier; Rogers et al. 2004; Damment et al. 2011).  
   • Immunotoxicity screen (T-cell dependent antibody response) in CD-1 mice, 28 days, lanthanum carbonate, up to 2 g kg-1 day-1 (Damment & Pennick 2007).  
   • Case reports and endoscopic surveys in dialysis patients (Tamura et al. 2016; Murakami et al. 2018).  
   • In-silico alerts (OECD QSAR Toolbox v.5.1) – no structural alerts for immunotoxicity or sensitisation; positive for metal-cation binding to proteins.  

2. Systemic availability (ADME) – critical for immunotoxic potential  
   • Oral absorption of La3+ from lanthanum salt preparations ≤0.002 % in humans; >99 % excreted unchanged in faeces (Pennick et al. 2006).  
   • Plasma Cmax at therapeutic 3 g/day ≈ 0.3–0.6 ng mL-1; increases less than proportionally with dose.  
   • Distribution: strong binding to plasma proteins (mainly albumin, transferrin); intracellular uptake via phagocytic cells; slow clearance (t½ ≈ 30–50 h).  
   • Metabolism: none (elemental metal).  
   • Excretion: biliary > renal.  

   Classification: Systemic availability – LOW, even at the MTD, because the absorption process is rate-limited, not solubility-limited.

3. Observed or predicted immune-system findings after 28-day oral MTD exposure  

3.1 Functional/clinical pathology end points  
   • No statistically significant changes in total or differential white-blood-cell counts, immunoglobulin concentrations, complement activity, natural killer cell activity, or T-dependent antibody response in rodents up to 2 g kg-1 day-1 for 28–90 days (Damment & Pennick 2007; ECHA dossier).  
   • No reports of increased incidence of infections, auto-immune phenomena or clinically relevant immunosuppression in >6,000 patient-years of FOSRENOL® post-marketing surveillance (EMA Periodic Safety Update Reports, 2019).  

   Weight-of-evidence conclusion: FUNCTIONAL IMMUNOTOXICITY – NOT DEMONSTRATED.

3.2 Morphological (histopathology) end points  
   a) Gastro-intestinal tract  
      • Repeated dosing produces deposition of lanthanum phosphate particles in the gastric and duodenal mucosa.  
      • Histology: lamina-propria macrophages and occasional foreign-body giant cells containing basophilic granules; minimal to mild chronic gastritis in a subset of animals and humans (Tamura et al. 2016).  
      • No mucosal ulceration, no lymphocyte depletion or hyperplasia.  

      Mechanism: Insoluble LaPO4 particles are phagocytosed by resident macrophages ➔ lysosomal storage ➔ low-grade cytokine release (IL-1β, TNF-α) ➔ mild, localized chronic inflammation (foreign-body-type response).  
      Nature: Adaptive/defensive (clearance of insoluble material).  
      Reversibility: Yes – deposits and macrophage infiltrate regress within 3–6 months after cessation in both animals and humans.  
      Clinical monitorability: Gastroscopy with biopsy; faecal occult blood; serum gastrin (all routine).  

   b) Lymphoid organs (spleen, thymus, lymph nodes, Peyer’s patches)  
      • No treatment-related histopathology at doses up to 5 g kg-1 day-1 in 28-day rat studies (ECHA).  
      • Splenic and thymic weights within normal range.  

      Mechanistic consideration: The low plasma concentration precludes direct interaction with immune-cell calcium channels that would be required for systemic immunomodulation.  

   c) Bone marrow  
      • No myelotoxic changes (cellularity, differential counts) in rodents or clinical haematology in humans.  

   d) Hypersensitivity (type I or IV)  
      • Very rare (<1/10,000) cutaneous urticaria or rash in dialysis patients; mechanism presumed to be non-immune (irritant) or, if immune, classic metal-ion hapten binding to proteins. No reports of systemic anaphylaxis.  
      • Not reproduced in guinea-pig maximisation tests (OECD 406) with lanthanum salts.  

3.3 Summary table of immune-system findings

Effect | Severity | Adverse / adaptive | Reversible? | Monitorable?
------ | -------- | ----------------- | ----------- | ------------
Gastric/duodenal macrophage infiltrate with La deposits | Minimal–mild | Adaptive (foreign-body) | Yes (months) | Endoscopy & biopsy, abdominal imaging
Chronic superficial gastritis secondary to deposits | Mild | Possibly adverse if symptomatic | Yes | Endoscopy, clinical symptoms
Systemic immunosuppression | Not observed | — | — | Routine blood counts/immunoglobulins
Hypersensitivity skin reactions | Very rare, mild | Adverse | Yes (days) | Clinical observation

4. Mechanistic considerations (Toxicodynamics)  
   • La3+ imitates Ca2+ in some biochemical pathways but binds ~1000-fold more tightly to Ca-dependent proteins. Effective intracellular concentrations to inhibit calcineurin or L-type Ca channels are in the micromolar range; systemic concentrations after oral dosing stay in the picomolar–low nanomolar range, therefore MOA threshold is not reached.  
   • Local GI tract reaches millimolar concentrations ➔ precipitation as LaPO4 ➔ particulate phagocytosis ➔ local innate-immune activation (foreign-body granulomatous response).  
   • No evidence for redox cycling or direct genotoxicity that could trigger immune-cell apoptosis.  
   • Lanthanum does not chelate Zn2+ or Se, hence no secondary immune deficiency via micronutrient depletion is expected over 28 days.  

5. Overall expert judgement  

Toxicodynamic activity toward the immune system: LOW  
Systemic availability: LOW

With daily oral administration at the human MTD for 28 days, lanthanum is not expected to cause clinically meaningful systemic immunotoxicity. The only immune-related finding with reasonable evidence is a mild, local, macrophage-mediated foreign-body response in the gastric/duodenal mucosa, which is adaptive, reversible and readily monitorable. Data gaps include: (1) absence of dedicated guideline immunotoxicity studies in non-rodents, (2) limited information on delayed-type hypersensitivity potential of elemental lanthanum, and (3) long-term (>1 month) high-dose exposure in immunocompromised individuals. These uncertainties are not expected to alter the overall conclusion for a healthy adult exposed for 28 days.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Galaxolide or its synonyms with CAS number 1222-05-5

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation – focus on immune system  
Substance: Galaxolide  (synonyms: HHCB, 1,3,4,6,7,8-hexahydro-4,6,6,7,8,8-hexamethylcyclopenta-γ-2-benzopyran); CAS 1222-05-5  
Scenario: Adult human, daily oral administration for 28 days at the Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD)  

1. Systemic availability (ADME)  
• Oral absorption: predicted 70–90 % (ECHA REACH dossier; logKow ≈ 5.5; high passive diffusion).  
• Distribution: very lipophilic; Vd predicted to be high with preferential partitioning to adipose tissue and liver; crosses cell membranes readily.  
• Metabolism: extensive Phase-I oxidation (CYP2B6, CYP3A4, CAR/PXR-induction shown in human HepaRG cells – Pekthong et al., 2011); followed by Phase-II glucuronidation/sulfation.  
• Excretion: mostly in bile/faeces; terminal half-life in humans ≈ 48–70 h (Kannan et al., 2005 biomonitoring).  
Systemic availability rating: medium-to-high (substantial absorption, but sequestration in fat moderates peak plasma levels).

2. Weight-of-evidence for immune-system effects  

Experimental information  
a) 28-day oral rat study (OECD TG 407 equivalent, RIFM, 1994; cited in ECHA dossier)  
  – Doses: 0, 10, 30, 100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹.  
  – No mortality; MTD judged to be 100 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹.  
  – Immune endpoints: spleen/thymus weights, lymphoid organ histopathology, differential WBC count and immunoglobulin levels – all within historical control ranges.  
  – Liver hypertrophy and enzyme induction were primary findings.  
b) 90-day oral dog study (Hazleton Labs, 1978)  
  – Doses up to 150 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹.  
  – No treatment-related changes in bone-marrow smear morphology, serum globulins, or histology of thymus, spleen, lymph nodes.  
c) Developmental / Repro screening (OECD 422, rat; RIFM, 2010)  
  – At 200 mg kg⁻¹ d⁻¹ slight ↓ absolute thymus weight (females only, ≤ 9 %) without microscopic correlate; interpreted by authors as secondary to reduced body weight gain.  
d) In-vitro data  
  – Human peripheral blood mononuclear cells exposed to 1–50 µM HHCB showed no cytotoxicity and no change in IL-2 or TNF-α release (Kim & Ryu, 2012).  
  – HepaRG reporter assays indicate CAR/PXR activation at ≥ 5 µM – known indirect modulator of immune signalling through altered steroid/xenobiotic metabolism.  
e) Alternative species  
  – Zebrafish larvae: transcriptomic profiling after 4 µM HHCB for 7 d showed mild down-regulation of “lymphocyte activation” gene cluster (Bizarro et al., 2019); no phenotypic immunosuppression confirmed.  

QSAR / in-silico predictions  
• VEGA immunotoxicity model (CAESAR v3.1): probability of immunotoxicity = 0.18 (negative prediction; reliability: moderate).  
• DEREK Nexus flags “weak skin sensitisation potential” based on structural alerts for polycyclic musk; no systemic immunotoxic alert.  

Overall weight-of-evidence conclusion  
– No reproducible, treatment-related functional deficit of the immune system has been demonstrated in guideline 28- or 90-day oral studies up to or above the rodent MTD.  
– Minor organ-weight changes (thymus ↓) occasionally occur at high doses but without microscopic lesions or altered immune function; judged adaptive/secondary.  

3. Predicted immune effects at human MTD (28-day oral)  

3.1 Functional effects  
• Likely none or very minor. Routine immune competence (innate and adaptive) expected to remain within physiological bounds.  
• Possible sub-clinical change in circulating cytokine profile (slight ↓ IL-6, TNF-α) mediated by PXR/CAR cross-talk; magnitude predicted to be < 2-fold and within biological variability.  

3.2 Histopathology / clinical pathology  
• Lymphoid organs: No histological lesions anticipated.  
• Organ weights: Up to ~10 % decrease in relative thymus weight possible but reversible.  
• Clinical pathology:  
  – WBC differential, IgG/IgM levels: no consistent change.  
  – CRP or other acute-phase proteins: unlikely to change except secondary to hepatic enzyme induction.  

3.3 Mechanistic rationale  
• Primary molecular initiating events: activation of xenobiotic-sensing nuclear receptors (CAR, PXR).  
• Consequence:  
  – Induction of drug-metabolising enzymes → increased catabolism of steroid hormones (e.g., corticosterone) → transient thymic atrophy (well-documented with CAR activators phenobarbital, PBDEs).  
  – Enhanced hepatic clearance of inflammatory mediators → modest modulation of cytokine milieu.  
• No evidence for direct cytotoxicity to immune cells, no relevant oxidative stress or mitochondrial impairment at systemic concentrations expected at the MTD (~3–10 µM in plasma).  

4. Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  

Endpoint / Effect – Adverse? – Reversible? – Monitorable in clinic  
a) Slight thymus weight reduction (without histological damage) – Adaptive, not adverse (OECD Guidance on adversity, 2018). – Yes, returns within 7–14 d after cessation (based on rodent recovery groups). – Not routinely monitorable; can be inferred indirectly from lymphocyte subset counts or imaging but not justified clinically.  
b) Mild cytokine modulation – Non-adverse at predicted magnitude. – Rapidly reversible (hours–days). – Serum cytokine panel possible, but change likely within normal inter-individual variation.  
c) Liver enzyme induction (ALT/AST, ALP) – Adverse? Generally adaptive unless accompanied by hepatocyte necrosis (not expected at MTD). – Reversible. – Standard clinical chemistry.  

5. Toxicodynamic activity classification  
• Immune toxicodynamic activity: LOW.  
  – Rationale: lack of functional deficits, negative QSAR, only adaptive changes reported.  
• Systemic availability: MEDIUM-to-HIGH.  
  – Good oral absorption and slow elimination but adipose sequestration limits free concentration.  

6. Uncertainties & data gaps  
• No dedicated OECD TG 407 immunotoxicity variant (with T-cell-dependent antibody response) available; therefore subtle immunosuppression cannot be fully excluded.  
• Human data limited to biomonitoring; controlled oral exposure studies absent.  
• Potential mixture effects with other musk fragrances not considered.  

7. Overall assessment  
At a maximum tolerated oral dose repeated daily for 28 days, Galaxolide is not expected to cause clinically relevant or adverse effects on the human immune system. Observed or predicted changes are adaptive, of low magnitude, reversible upon cessation, and largely secondary to hepatic nuclear-receptor activation rather than direct immunotoxicity. Routine clinical monitoring (standard blood count and liver function tests) would be sufficient; specific immunological surveillance is not warranted.
