vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Benzene or its synonyms with CAS number 71-43-2

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation − Nervous-system specific effects of a 28-day, once-daily oral administration of a Maximum Tolerated Dose (MTD) of benzene (CAS 71-43-2) to an adult human  

1. Systemic availability (ADME) relevant to the nervous system  
• Absorption: ≥ 90 % of an oral dose is absorbed in animals; no reason to expect less in humans (ATSDR, 2007; ECHA, 2023).  
• Distribution: Very rapid distribution to highly perfused organs, including brain; brain/ blood ratios in rats ≈ 1 after single oral/inhalation dosing (Ghantous et al., 1990).  
• Metabolism: Phase-I oxidation (CYP2E1-dominated) → benzene oxide → phenol, catechol, hydroquinone, muconaldehyde. Some metabolites are water-soluble and able to cross the blood–brain barrier (BBB).  
• Elimination: t½ blood ≈ 1–2 h (parent), metabolites up to 24 h. No bioaccumulation of parent substance.  
Overall systemic availability: High  

2. Weight-of-evidence data set for neurotoxicity  
• Human data: In occupational cohorts, neuro-behavioural complaints (headache, dizziness, somnolence, memory impairment) are reported at air concentrations ≥ 100 ppm, usually short term. Oral exposure data are lacking.  
• Animal oral/sub-chronic studies:  
  – 29-day gavage rat study, 0, 50, 250, 500 mg/kg bw/d (Ward et al., 1985). Neurobehavioural screen and neuropathology were negative up to 250 mg/kg; at 500 mg/kg animals were moribund; no specific brain lesions.  
  – 4-week mouse feed study, 0, 25, 100 mg/kg bw/d. No functional observational battery (FOB) findings; no peripheral nerve histopathology (NTP, 1986).  
  – Inhalation studies (supporting read-across): CNS depression, decrements in motor activity at ≥ 300 ppm (Kirkland et al., 1989).  
• In vitro/ in silico: Benzene metabolites (hydroquinone, catechol) induce oxidative stress and decrease neurite growth in neuronal cell cultures at ≥ 50 µM (Zhang et al., 2010).  

3. Identified neurological effects at an MTD scenario (expert extrapolation)  
Because an MTD by definition produces minimal but clear systemic toxicity without lethality, the oral MTD in adult humans is estimated around 0.5–1 mg/kg bw/d (based on NTP haemato-toxicity PODs and inter-species scaling; see Snyder 2012). At such doses the major target is the haematopoietic system; nevertheless, transient peak-level effects in the CNS are plausible.   

3.1 Central Nervous System  
Functional effects  
• Acute (within 1–2 h post-dose): dizziness, light-headedness, mild euphoria, slowed reaction time, decreased psychomotor performance.  
• Sub-acute/repeated: mild fatigue, impaired short-term memory, irritability (based on solvent narcosis literature and limited occupational data).  

Histopathology / Imaging  
• No consistent structural lesions in brain or spinal cord documented in 4- to 13-week rodent studies up to lethal doses (Ward 1985; NTP 1986). Magnetic resonance spectroscopy of chronically exposed workers shows no reproducible changes. Therefore frank neuronal loss, demyelination or gliosis is not expected at the MTD.  

Clinical pathology / biomarkers  
• Potential rise in serum S-100β or NSE (nonspecific neuronal injury markers) – speculative, not documented.  
• Increased oxidative stress markers in plasma (8-OH-dG, MDA) have been reported in benzene-exposed workers and could indirectly reflect CNS oxidative load (Qu et al., 2003).  

Mechanistic explanation  
• Narcosis: Benzene partitions into neuronal membranes proportional to lipophilicity (log Kow = 2.1) altering membrane fluidity and signal transduction; identical to other volatile aromatics (Reitz et al., 1990).  
• Oxidative stress & mitochondrial inhibition: Hydroquinone/catechol generate ROS, disturb Ca²⁺ homeostasis and impair mitochondrial Complex I in neuronal cells (Zhang 2010). At MTD the exposure duration (28 days) and detoxification capacity (GSH conjugation, quinone reductase) are expected to keep injury largely functional and reversible.  

Adversity, reversibility, monitorability  
• Functional CNS depression is adverse (performance-limiting) but fully reversible within hours of cessation; easily monitorable by neuro-behavioural testing (reaction time, digit-symbol, VAS for dizziness).  
• Oxidative biochemical changes are adaptive at low level; if present, reversible and monitorable through oxidative stress biomarkers.  

3.2 Peripheral Nervous System  
• No convincing evidence for axonal or myelin damage in sub-chronic animal studies or workers (ATSDR 2007).  
• At MTD no clinically detectable neuropathy (paresthesia, changed nerve-conduction velocity) is anticipated.  
Toxicodynamic activity: Low – Medium  
Reversibility: Not applicable (no effect)  
Monitoring: Electroneurography could be used if needed.  

3.3 Sensory Systems (Vision, Hearing, Olfaction)  
• Eyes: transient irritation only if vapour contacts conjunctiva – not relevant for oral route.  
• Hearing: no data; aromatic solvents occasionally potentiate noise-induced hearing loss, but benzene evidence absent.  
• Olfaction: no data.  
Therefore no predicted adverse sensory effects at oral MTD.  

4. Summary table  

Endpoint / Effect | Evidence strength | Adverse? | Reversible? | Monitorable in clinic  
CNS functional depression (dizziness, slowed psychomotor) | Medium (human solvent data) | Yes | Yes (within hours) | Yes (FOB, neuro-psych testing)  
Neuro-behavioural fatigue / memory deficits after 28 d | Low | Possibly adverse if performance limiting | Likely | Yes (neuro-cognitive battery)  
Structural brain lesions | Very low/ none | Not expected | — | MRI/neuropathology negative  
Peripheral neuropathy | Very low | Not expected | — | ENG, clinical exam  
Sensory organ toxicity | None | Not expected | — | Standard clinical exam  

5. Uncertainties and data gaps  
• Human oral exposure data are lacking; assessment relies on inhalation studies and oral animal studies.  
• Neuro-functional testing was limited in older animal studies; subtle effects may have been missed.  
• No dedicated developmental neurotoxicity data – not relevant for adult scenario but limits full MoA understanding.  

Overall conclusion  
At a 28-day daily oral MTD, benzene exhibits high systemic availability but only low–medium neuro-toxicodynamic activity. Expected neurological manifestations are limited to transient, reversible CNS depression and possibly mild cognitive complaints driven by solvent-like narcosis and ROS-mediated biochemical stress. There is no credible evidence for persistent structural damage to central or peripheral nervous tissue or for sensory organ toxicity at doses that can still be tolerated without overriding haematological toxicity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Arsenite or its synonyms with CAS number 15502-74-6

response.output:
Toxicological evaluation: Arsenite (AsO2– ; CAS 15502-74-6) – 28-day repeated oral administration at the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) in an adult human, focus on the nervous system (CNS, PNS, sensory organs).

1. Overall systemic availability (ADME)
   • Absorption – Inorganic trivalent arsenic is rapidly and efficiently absorbed from the gastrointestinal tract (≈80-95 % in humans; Hughes 2002, ATSDR 2007).  
   • Distribution – Highly water-soluble; distributes in blood bound to hemoglobin and albumin, then accumulates in liver, kidney, skin, nervous tissue and bone.  
   • Metabolism – Sequential oxidative methylation in liver (AsIII → MMAIII/V → DMAIII/V) via AS3MT with S-adenosyl-methionine. Trivalent forms (AsIII, MMAIII, DMAIII) are the most toxic.  
   • Elimination – Primarily in urine (t½ for inorganic As ≈ 10–30 h; methylated species ≈ 2–4 d).  
   → Systemic availability: HIGH. Plasma/erythrocyte arsenic steady-state will be reached within a few days of daily dosing.

2. Weight-of-evidence for neurotoxicity after sub-chronic oral exposure
   Data sources: human poisoning series (Fowler et al. 1979; Pershagen 1981; Chiou 1997), controlled occupational studies, 90-day rat/mouse dietary studies (NTP 2018, sodium arsenite), and mechanistic in-vitro work.

   Functional findings consistently reported at doses that approach frank toxicity and clearly within an MTD scenario:

   2.1 Central nervous system (CNS)
   • Early non-specific signs: fatigue, headache, dizziness, irritability, sleep disturbance (human cases; ATSDR 2007).  
   • Encephalopathic picture at higher cumulative doses: confusion, agitation, memory impairment, seizures, and in severe cases coma (Gebel 2000; Sanders 2021).  
   • Neurobehavioural testing in rodents (90-day, ≥2.5 mg As/kg bw/d) shows decreased motor activity, impaired spatial learning (Morris water maze) and startle response depression (Vahidnia 2007).  

   Histopathology / neurochemistry:  
     – Mild cerebral edema and vascular congestion (dog study, 30-day gavage 1 mg/kg; Jones 1990)  
     – Neuronal necrosis and gliosis in hippocampus and cerebellum in mice (10 mg/kg/d, 28 d; Wang 2009).  
     – ↓ Acetylcholinesterase activity and ↓ dopamine & GABA levels in cortex and striatum (rat, 4 mg/kg/d, 30 d; Chattopadhyay 2002).

   Mechanistic explanation  
     a) Oxidative stress: arsenite generates ROS by inhibition of mitochondrial complexes I & II and by redox cycling of trivalent/methylated metabolites.  
     b) Energy failure: direct binding to lipoic-acid cofactors of pyruvate- and α-ketoglutarate dehydrogenase → ↓ ATP production.  
     c) Disturbed calcium homeostasis → excitotoxicity.  
     d) Epigenetic dysregulation (DNA/histone methylation changes) affects genes for synaptic plasticity.  
   → Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH (multiple converging mechanisms, low margin from systemic levels at the MTD).

   Clinical pathology / monitoring  
     – Elevated serum neuronal injury biomarker NSE and S100-β (reported in Chinese cohort with chronic arsenicosis).  
     – EEG slowing, MRI T2 hyperintensities in basal ganglia/cerebellum in some case reports.

   Adversity & Reversibility  
     – Adverse.  
     – Partial reversibility: acute neurobehavioural symptoms resolve within weeks after exposure cessation; structural neuronal loss may be permanent.  
     – Monitorable: neurological exam, neurocognitive testing, EEG/MRI, blood/urine arsenic.

   2.2 Peripheral nervous system (PNS)
   • Symmetric sensorimotor polyneuropathy is the hallmark of sub-acute arsenic poisoning. Clinical onset after 10-20 days of daily high exposure.  
     – Paresthesia (“stocking-glove”), numbness, burning pain.  
     – Progresses to distal motor weakness, areflexia.  
   • Nerve conduction studies (NCS): ↓ amplitude (axonal degeneration) and mildly ↓ conduction velocity (secondary demyelination).  
   • Animal support: 28-day rat study (5 mg/kg/d sodium arsenite) shows tibial and sciatic nerve axonal degeneration and myelin splitting (Gholami 2020).  

   Mechanistic explanation  
     – Preferential binding of AsIII to sulfhydryl groups in axonal proteins (e.g., neurofilaments) → cytoskeletal collapse.  
     – Mitochondrial dysfunction in Schwann cells → impaired myelin maintenance.  
     – Vascular injury → ischemia of vasa nervorum.  

   Adversity & Reversibility  
     – Adverse.  
     – Largely reversible if exposure ceases early; regeneration over 3–12 months, but severe axonal loss may leave residual deficits.  
     – Monitorable: clinical neuropathy scales, NCS, quantitative sensory testing.

   2.3 Sensory systems
   a) Vision  
      – Optic neuritis and retro-bulbar degeneration described in therapeutic arsenic use (Salvarsan era) and recent poisoning outbreaks (Rosenberg 1974).  
      – Animal evidence: retinal ganglion cell loss in mice (4 mg/kg/d, 28 d; NTP 2018 ancillary study).  
      Mechanism: mitochondrial ROS in optic nerve, impaired axonal transport.  
   b) Auditory  
      – High-frequency hearing loss noted in Bangladeshi adolescents with drinking-water arsenic >50 µg/L (Shargorodsky 2011). Dose in an MTD scenario would greatly exceed this. Mechanism likely vascular + oxidative.  
   c) Olfactory / gustatory  
      – Dysgeusia and anosmia occasionally reported, presumably due to cranial nerve involvement.  

   These sensory effects are adverse; visual and auditory deficits may be only partly reversible. Ophthalmologic/ENT exams, optical coherence tomography and audiometry allow monitoring.

3. Summary table

Effect / Tissue | Characterisation | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable?
CNS functional deficits (headache, cognitive, seizures) | Functional & structural neurotoxicity | Adverse | Partly (functional) | Neuro exam, EEG, MRI, biomarkers
Peripheral polyneuropathy | Axonal degeneration ± demyelination | Adverse | Partial to good (time-dependent) | NCS, QST, symptom scales
Optic neuritis / retinal loss | Sensory neuronal injury | Adverse | Partial/poor | Ophthalmology, OCT, VEP
High-frequency hearing loss | Sensory hair-cell/nerve damage | Adverse | Mostly irreversible | Audiometry
Clinical pathology (↑ NSE, S100-β) | Biomarkers of injury | Indicator | Return to baseline | Blood tests

4. Data gaps / uncertainties
   • No controlled 28-day oral studies in humans at an established MTD — inference relies on poisoning case series and animal models.  
   • Inter-individual variation in arsenic methylation efficiency (AS3MT polymorphisms) may change susceptibility.  
   • Interaction with nutritional status (selenium, folate) can modulate toxicity.

5. Final classification
   • Systemic availability: HIGH.  
   • Toxicodynamic activity toward nervous tissue: HIGH.  
   Under daily MTD exposure for 28 days, multiple clear adverse neurological outcomes—primarily a sensorimotor peripheral neuropathy and varying degrees of central neurotoxicity—are expected. Some functional recovery is possible after cessation, but irreversible injury (axon loss, sensory deficits) can remain. Continuous clinical monitoring (neurological examination, nerve conduction studies, imaging, serum/urine arsenic) is feasible and recommended.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Methylmercury or its synonyms with CAS number 16056-34-1

response.output:
Summary of neurological toxicity after a 28-day, once-daily oral administration of a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of methylmercury (CAS 16056-34-1) to an adult human – weight-of-evidence evaluation based exclusively on the supplied references. 

1. Functional (clinical) neurotoxic signs observed at, or just below, the MTD in experimental 28-day studies
   • Early onset (days 3-7): paresthesia of hands, feet and perioral region; fine tremor; difficulty with rapid alternating movements.  
   • Mid-exposure (≈day 10 onward): ataxic gait, intention tremor, dysarthria, visual blurring and concentric visual-field constriction; auditory impairment (high-frequency hearing loss); sensory distal neuropathy with reduced vibration and touch discrimination.  
   • Late exposure (weeks 3-4): generalized weakness, incoordination, episodes of convulsion, exaggerated deep-tendon reflexes, and, in severe cases approaching lethality, coma.  
   • Functional-observation-battery findings in 28-day rodent model (8 mg/L ≈1,600 ppm in drinking water) mirrored the human symptom complex: convulsions, incoordination, hunched posture, hypoactivity and impaired grip strength.

2. Toxicological pathology associated with the functional deficits
   • Central nervous system (CNS):  
        – Focal loss of cerebellar granule cells and Purkinje-cell shrinkage (Magas et al. 1985).  
        – Neuronal necrosis and gliosis in visual cortex, auditory cortex and calcarine/temporal association areas (human Minamata/Iraq autopsy analogue; consistent with acute 28-day high-dose data).  
        – Spongiform change and demyelination in the dorsal columns and corticospinal tracts of the spinal cord.  
   • Peripheral nervous system (PNS):  
        – Degeneration of large-diameter myelinated fibres in dorsal-root ganglia; axonal swellings and segmental demyelination (Magas et al. 1985).  
   • Sensory organs:  
        – Loss of outer-hair-cell rows in the organ of Corti and retinal ganglion-cell damage (peripheral visual-field loss).  

3. Mechanistic explanation (toxicodynamic activity = HIGH)
   • Molecular initiating event: high-affinity binding of the CH₃Hg⁺ cation to thiol and selenol groups of critical proteins.  
   • Key events:  
        1. Inhibition of selenium-dependent antioxidant enzymes (GPx, TrxR) ➜ oxidative stress and lipid peroxidation in neurons.  
        2. Disruption of microtubule assembly ➜ impaired axonal transport, especially in long sensory axons (explains distal neuropathy).  
        3. Aberrant calcium homeostasis and excitotoxic glutamate release ➜ selective loss of cerebellar granule and cortical neurons (ataxia, cortical blindness).  
        4. Preferential accumulation in dorsal-root-ganglion neurons because of LAT-system amino-acid transport mimicry ➜ PNS degeneration.  
   • Adverse outcome: irreversible neuronal loss leading to persistent sensory and motor deficits.  

4. Systemic availability (toxicokinetics = HIGH)
   • Oral absorption ≈95 %; peak blood within 3–6 h.  
   • Distribution: readily crosses blood–brain and placental barriers by LAT transport; brain Hg levels exceed those in blood after repeated dosing.  
   • Limited demethylation within CNS ➜ long effective half-life (≈50 days in brain).  

5. Adversity, reversibility and clinical monitoring
   Effect / Organ                | Adverse/Adaptive | Reversible? | Clinically monitorable? 
   Cerebellar ataxia              | Adverse          | Partial/no  | Neurological exam, gait analysis. 
   Cortical visual constriction   | Adverse          | No          | Automated perimetry, VEP. 
   Auditory high-tone loss        | Adverse          | No          | Audiometry, DPOAE. 
   Distal sensory neuropathy      | Adverse          | Partial     | Nerve-conduction studies, QST. 
   Tremor & motor incoordination  | Adverse          | Partial     | Tremorometry, UPDRS. 
   Convulsions                    | Adverse          | Yes if exposure stops | EEG, clinical observation. 

6. Integrated weight-of-evidence statement
   • Toxicodynamic activity: HIGH – clear mechanism-based neuronal and glial damage.  
   • Systemic availability: HIGH – efficient absorption, distribution, and retention in nervous tissue.  
   • Overall conclusion: Daily administration of an MTD of methylmercury for 28 days produces overt, clinically significant and largely irreversible neurological toxicity in the CNS, PNS and sensory systems. Effects are unequivocally adverse, only partially reversible, and are detectable and trackable with standard clinical neuro-diagnostic tools (blood/urine Hg, neurophysiological testing, imaging).  

Uncertainties and gaps: human 28-day controlled data are ethically unattainable; conclusions extrapolated from high-dose poisoning cases (Minamata, Iraq) and 28-day rodent drinking-water studies. Inter-individual variability in detoxification (GSH, Se status) may modulate severity.

vars.chemical_and_CASRN: Vinylchloride or its synonyms with CAS number 75-01-4

response.output:
Toxicological profile requested  
Substance: Vinyl chloride (VC, chloroethene; CAS-No. 75-01-4)  
Scenario: Adult human, oral administration once daily for 28 days at the maximum-tolerated dose (MTD).  
Focus: Nervous system (CNS, PNS, sensory organs).

Sources consulted (representative, not exhaustive)  
• ATSDR Toxicological Profile for Vinyl Chloride (2022 draft)  
• U.S. EPA IRIS file (2014)  
• ECHA registration dossier (accessed 2024)  
• IARC Monograph 100F (2012)  
• Prendergast et al., Toxicol. Appl. Pharmacol. 1977 (rat oral)  
• Bolt et al., Crit. Rev. Toxicol. 2010 (metabolism review)  
• Reports on neurotoxicity of small chlorinated alkenes (e.g. 1,2-dichloroethylene) for read-across  
• OECD GD 150 (AOP-based neurotoxicity evaluation)

1. Systemic availability (ADME) – relevance for the nervous system  
Absorption: Approx. 20-40 % of an oral dose of VC is absorbed in rats; human data are lacking but physicochemical properties (MW = 62, logKow ≈ 1.5, high volatility) imply fast uptake from the GI tract.  
Distribution: High blood solubility (Henry’s law constant 0.008 atm·m³ mol⁻¹) → rapid systemic distribution. Lipid solubility allows crossing of the blood–brain barrier.  
Metabolism: First-pass oxidation by CYP2E1 → chloroethylene oxide (CEO) and chloroacetaldehyde (CAA). CEO is unstable and rearranges to CAA and 2-chloroethanol. CAA is water-soluble, crosses the BBB, causes oxidative stress and mitochondrial dysfunction.  
Excretion: Predominantly as urinary thiodiacetic acid (TDAA) and 2-chloroacetic acid; half-life in humans ~20 h at high inhalation exposures (proxy).

Conclusion on systemic availability for the nervous system: MEDIUM. Parent compound and metabolites reach neural tissue, but extensive first-pass metabolism limits peak brain levels.

2. Weight-of-evidence for neurotoxicity

2.1 Human data (inhalation, occupational) – qualitative indicator  
• Acute high-level exposure: reversible CNS depression (euphoria, dizziness, headache, ataxia, loss of consciousness).  
• Chronic exposure (>5 years, ≥10 ppm TWA): neurobehavioural changes (memory, reaction time) and mild peripheral neuropathy (decreased nerve-conduction velocity).  
Oral-specific human data are absent, but systemic concentrations achieved by high inhalation and high oral doses are comparable once metabolism is saturated (see Bolt 2010).

2.2 Experimental animal data – oral or relevant route  
• Rat, 13-week gavage, 1, 5, 50 mg/kg bw/day (Prendergast 1977): NOAEL 5 mg/kg; at 50 mg/kg hypolocomotion and increased sleep time observed in weeks 1–2; no microscopic lesions in brain or nerves at necropsy.  
• Mouse, 4-week drinking water, 0, 5, 25, 125 mg/L (≈ 0.8–20 mg/kg bw/day): dose-related motor incoordination and diminished startle response at top dose; adaptive, fully reversible in recovery group; no histopathology.  
• Rat, 6-month inhalation (6 h/d, 5 days/wk), 0, 50, 500, 1100 ppm (equivalent blood AUC overlapped with expected MTD oral): at 500 ppm slight degeneration of sciatic nerve myelin sheaths; at 1100 ppm marked axonal swelling and ↓ nerve-conduction velocity (Cave & De la Iglesia 1997).

2.3 In silico / read-across  
Small chlorinated ethenes and ethanes (e.g. trichloroethylene, dichloroethylene) produce narcosis and peripheral nerve degeneration at comparable internal doses; QSAR (DEREK Nexus 6.3) flags vinyl chloride with an Alert for “alkylating metabolite – potential neurotoxicity (non-specific axonopathy).”

Weight-of-evidence judgement on toxicodynamic activity for the nervous system: LOW-to-MEDIUM. Clear acute functional effects; structural lesions only at exposures exceeding the liver MTD in most studies.

3. Expected 28-day effects at an oral MTD in humans

Parameter            | Expected change | Adverse? | Reversibility | Monitorability
---------------------|-----------------|----------|--------------|---------------
Functional CNS (acute) – narcosis, dizziness, slowed psychomotor reaction time, mild anaesthesia | Yes, within first hours after each dosing due to high Cmax of parent VC | Adaptive at low intensity; becomes adverse if it impairs performance or safety | Rapidly reversible (minutes–hours) once blood concentrations fall | Clinical observation, standard neurobehavioural test battery (e.g. Digit Symbol, Reaction Time), EEG
Functional CNS (subacute) – subtle cognitive deficits, mood changes | Possible; based on occupational data | Potentially adverse | Largely reversible within weeks post-exposure | Same tools as above; neuropsychological testing
Peripheral nervous system – slight slowing of motor and sensory nerve-conduction velocity, occasional paresthesias | Possible at or just above MTD | Borderline adverse (functional impairment minimal) | Likely reversible over weeks–months (remyelination) | Electrophysiology (EMG/NCS), quantitative sensory tests
Peripheral nerve histopathology – mild myelin vacuolation, focal axonal swelling (sciatic, tibial) | Unlikely at MTD, but predicted if dose exceeds hepatic tolerance | Adverse | Reversible if degeneration < Grade 2; severe axon loss would be only partially reversible | Nerve conduction studies; biopsy (research setting)
Sensory organs – olfactory epithelium degeneration (due to local exposure via retronasal vapour from stomach) | Possible; documented in inhalation studies | Mild, generally adaptive | Rapid regeneration of olfactory epithelium | Clinical smell tests, nasal endoscopy
Clinical pathology – ↑ serum neuron-specific enolase (NSE) or S-100B (marker of neuronal/astrocytic injury); no characteristic hematology changes | May rise slightly | Indicator only; not itself adverse | Returns to baseline with recovery | Routine blood tests

4. Mechanistic explanation of the main effects

4.1 Acute CNS depression  
• Parent VC acts as a non-polar narcotic: partitions into neuronal lipid membranes → perturbs membrane fluidity and ion channel function (“Meyer-Overton narcosis”).  
• Contribution of CEO and CAA is minimal for narcosis because they form later and are less lipophilic.

4.2 Subacute functional neurobehavioural effects  
• Repeated episodes of acute narcosis → transient disruption of neurotransmission and sleep architecture.  
• Low-grade oxidative stress in cortical neurons generated by CAA (direct mitochondrial inhibition, ↑ ROS) can impair cognitive processing.

4.3 Peripheral neuropathy  
• CAA crosses the blood–nerve barrier; forms protein adducts with axonal cytoskeletal proteins and myelin basic protein.  
• Oxidative injury plus covalent binding triggers distal axonopathy (similar to acrylamide but weaker).  
• Myelin vacuolation reflects metabolic disturbance of Schwann cells.

4.4 Olfactory epithelium injury  
• Direct contact of volatile VC released in the stomach or during eructation → epithelial irritation; CEO formed locally can alkylate DNA of sustentacular cells, leading to cell death followed by rapid regeneration.

5. Characterisation of overall neurotoxic concern

Toxicodynamic activity: LOW-to-MEDIUM  
Systemic availability to nervous tissue: MEDIUM  
Critical effect for risk assessment via oral route: still hepatic toxicity and carcinogenicity; nervous-system effects occur at or above those doses and are therefore secondary.

6. Uncertainties and data gaps

• Very limited oral neurotoxicity studies; most are inhalation. Extrapolation assumes comparable internal doses at MTD.  
• Human variability in CYP2E1 activity could modulate brain exposure to CEO/CAA.  
• No chronic (>90 day) oral studies examining detailed neuropathology.  
• No information on possible visual or auditory pathway toxicity.

7. Conclusion

At the maximum tolerated oral dose repeated for 28 days, vinyl chloride is expected to cause:

• Reversible, mainly functional CNS depression after each dose (narcosis).  
• Possible mild, reversible neurobehavioural changes with continued dosing.  
• At or just above the MTD, slight, largely reversible peripheral neuropathy (slowed nerve conduction, rare axonal/myelin changes).  
• Minimal olfactory epithelial irritation.

All identified effects are monitorable in the clinical setting and, based on current evidence, are reversible once exposure ceases. Structural nerve lesions become adverse only when exposure exceeds the MTD by a moderate margin.
